notes.purpose: Canonical affiliate registry for BenjiStack. This is the single source of truth for approved links and usage guidance.
notes.usageRule: Recommend tools only when they genuinely fit the business type and phase. Relevance beats monetization.
notes.decisionFramework: Start from the user phase and actual job-to-be-done. Identify the most useful tool(s) for that need. Prefer tools with approved affiliate links when they are genuinely good fits. If the best useful option has no affiliate link yet, still recommend it honestly and treat it as a candidate program to pursue.
notes.recommendationRule: Never recommend a tool only because it has an affiliate link. Utility and fit come first.
notes.comparisonRule: When two tools both fit and both are useful, recommend both with a short explanation of who each is better for.
notes.linkPresentationRule: Whenever an approved affiliate tool name is shown in BenjiStack-facing recommendations, the tool name itself should carry the affiliate link so clicks on the tool name can redirect through the affiliate URL, not only separate CTA text.

programs.name: GoHighLevel

labels.secondary: Start the trial + guided live setup

programs.whenToUse: Use when the founder wants one system instead of stitching together multiple tools.

linkRule.usePrimaryWhen: Presenting GHL as the straightforward all-in-one trial option.
linkRule.useSecondaryWhen: Guided onboarding, launch support, or beginner hand-holding would materially help.

programs.summary: All-in-one platform for pages, funnels, CRM, forms, bookings, follow-up, payments, and automation.
notIdealFor: simple one-page validation sites
people who only need a basic newsletter tool
classic multi-product ecommerce stores
jobsToBeDone: capture leads with forms and funnels
launch an all-in-one online selling setup
howToUseForBusiness: Connect the domain and launch a landing page or funnel quickly.
Add forms, calendars, and pipeline stages so leads are tracked automatically.
Set up email/SMS follow-up for inquiries, no-shows, and nurturing.
Use it when the business is operationally complex enough that replacing 4-6 tools is actually helpful.
specificScenarios: A lead-gen business that needs landing pages, forms, CRM, and automated follow-up in one stack.
A service business selling booked calls instead of products in a storefront.
A founder who wants one operational hub instead of stitching together separate page, CRM, and automation tools.
comparisonNotes: Compared with Shopify: choose GHL when the business is funnel-led, service-led, or follow-up-heavy rather than a classic store.
Compared with beehiiv: choose GHL for funnels/CRM/automation; choose beehiiv for newsletter/media businesses.
Compared with Carrd: GHL is heavier but much more capable once forms, CRM, bookings, and automation matter.
programs.recommendationStyle: Present GHL as the strong all-in-one option when consolidation truly helps. Do not force it into cases where a lighter tool is enough.

programs.name: beehiiv
programs.whenToUse: Use when newsletter or audience-building is core to the business model, not as a generic email recommendation for every business.

linkRule.usePrimaryWhen: The business model depends on newsletter publishing, audience growth, referrals, or sponsorship potential.

programs.summary: Newsletter-first platform for publishing, audience building, referral growth, and later monetization.
notIdealFor: people who mainly need CRM and funnels
founders who do not plan to build an email audience
pure ecommerce as the main model
howToUseForBusiness: Use beehiiv as the core newsletter home when the business depends on email audience growth.
Create the publication, signup flow, and welcome sequence first.
Publish consistently and use the platform to grow toward referrals, sponsorships, or paid recommendations later.
specificScenarios: A founder turning niche research into a weekly newsletter business.
A content + affiliate business that needs email capture plus long-term audience ownership.
A business using free content and newsletter trust to support affiliate monetization later.
comparisonNotes: Compared with Kit: beehiiv is stronger when the model feels like a media/newsletter business.
Compared with GHL: beehiiv is the better fit when the core asset is the newsletter, not the funnel/CRM stack.
programs.recommendationStyle: Recommend beehiiv when newsletter publishing and audience-building are central to the business model, not as a generic email tool for everything.

programs.name: Shopify
programs.whenToUse: Use when the business clearly needs a real storefront and ecommerce backend.

linkRule.usePrimaryWhen: The offer is product-led and needs catalog, cart, checkout, and store infrastructure.

programs.summary: Ecommerce platform for running a real online store with products, catalog, cart, and checkout.
notIdealFor: service businesses selling calls
newsletter-first media businesses
simple one-off digital offer funnels where storefront complexity is unnecessary
jobsToBeDone: take payments through a standard storefront checkout
howToUseForBusiness: Set up the store, products, collections, checkout, and basic brand pages.
Use Shopify when browsing, catalog structure, and standard ecommerce behavior matter.
Pair it later with email, ads, or content once the store foundation is working.
specificScenarios: A founder selling physical products online with more than one SKU.
A niche product business that needs a normal storefront rather than a lead funnel.
A business that needs product pages, cart, checkout, and order handling as the core experience.
comparisonNotes: Compared with GHL: choose Shopify for a true store; choose GHL for funnel-led/service-led selling and follow-up.
Compared with ThriveCart: choose Shopify for stores/catalogs; choose ThriveCart for digital offers and optimized checkout flows.
programs.recommendationStyle: Recommend Shopify when the business is genuinely store-led. Do not force it on service or newsletter businesses.

programs.name: Bluehost
programs.whenToUse: Use only when simple beginner hosting is the real need.

linkRule.usePrimaryWhen: The recommendation is specifically about basic hosting or a simple starter site.

programs.summary: Beginner-friendly hosting/domain option for getting a simple website online quickly.
idealFor: beginners who want a simple hosting path
people who need domain + hosting basics
notIdealFor: founders who only need a one-page Carrd site
audience-first newsletter businesses
cases where a registrar-only choice or an all-in-one funnel platform is better
jobsToBeDone: register a domain and set up hosting
get a basic starter website live
howToUseForBusiness: Use Bluehost when the bottleneck is simple hosting, not advanced sales or funnel logic.
Recommend it for basic website infrastructure when the user needs more than a one-page site but less than a complex stack.
Keep the recommendation simple and practical rather than hyped.
specificScenarios: A beginner who wants a basic company website and domain in one place.
An early business that needs simple web presence before more advanced tooling.
comparisonNotes: Compared with Carrd: choose Carrd for the fastest simple landing page; choose Bluehost when real hosting/site setup is the main need.
Compared with Namecheap/Hostinger later: use the tool that best matches registrar-only vs hosting-inclusive needs.
programs.recommendationStyle: Use sparingly. This is a useful infrastructure recommendation, not a centerpiece of the BenjiStack tool stack.

programs.name: InVideo
programs.whenToUse: Use when video is part of the acquisition or content strategy.

linkRule.usePrimaryWhen: The business needs fast video output for content, promotion, or audience growth.

programs.summary: Fast video creation tool for promo assets, social content, and simple marketing videos.
idealFor: operators who need simple promo videos without a full editing workflow
notIdealFor: businesses with no video strategy
founders who only need written/email assets
cases where video is not part of distribution
jobsToBeDone: support distribution with lightweight video assets
howToUseForBusiness: Create short product explainers, offer promos, social clips, or supporting content for distribution channels.
Use it when video helps the business get attention but a full production workflow would be overkill.
Treat it as a growth/distribution tool, not a core launch platform.
specificScenarios: A founder using short-form video to promote a newsletter or offer.
A digital product business making simple launch/promo videos.
A content business repackaging ideas into visual distribution assets.
comparisonNotes: Compared with Canva: Canva is better for static assets; InVideo is better when video itself is the channel.
programs.recommendationStyle: Recommend only when video is a real part of the go-to-market plan.

programs.name: Make
programs.whenToUse: Use only after the business has a real repeatable workflow to automate; not as a default beginner recommendation.

linkRule.usePrimaryWhen: The business already has multiple tools or repetitive operational steps that would benefit from automation.

programs.summary: Workflow automation tool for connecting apps and automating repeatable operational steps.
notIdealFor: true beginners with no real workflow yet
businesses that can still run simply by hand
people looking for their first launch tool
jobsToBeDone: connect forms, CRMs, sheets, and notifications
howToUseForBusiness: Start only after the manual process is clear; do not automate chaos.
Use Make to connect lead capture, internal tracking, alerts, CRM steps, or directory workflows once they are repeatable.
Frame it as an operations multiplier, not as a beginner launch requirement.
specificScenarios: A lead-gen business routing form leads into a CRM and alert system.
A directory business moving submissions into review and publishing workflows.
A founder syncing repetitive tasks between tools after the business is already working.
comparisonNotes: Compared with GHL: use GHL when one platform can handle the workflow natively; use Make when multiple tools need to be connected.
Do not position Make as the first thing a beginner should buy.
programs.recommendationStyle: Be explicit that Make is for later-stage workflow automation, not default beginner setup.

programs.name: Carrd
programs.whenToUse: Use when someone needs the fastest, simplest one-page site or validation landing page without heavier tooling.

linkRule.usePrimaryWhen: The goal is to get a simple landing page or waitlist page live quickly and cheaply.

programs.summary: Very lightweight one-page site builder for landing pages, waitlists, and simple validation sites.
notIdealFor: complex multi-page sites
businesses needing deep funnel/CRM automation
full ecommerce stores
jobsToBeDone: launch a simple landing page fast
put a one-page offer online cheaply
howToUseForBusiness: Use Carrd to test positioning, capture emails, or publish a simple offer page fast.
Recommend it when speed and simplicity matter more than advanced funnels or deep site structure.
It is often the right answer when the founder is overcomplicating the first launch.
specificScenarios: A founder testing a waitlist before building the product.
A simple service or digital offer that only needs one strong page and a CTA.
An early validation page before investing in a larger stack.
comparisonNotes: Compared with Framer: Carrd is usually simpler and lighter; Framer is better when polish/design flexibility matters more.
Compared with GHL: choose Carrd for the lean first page; choose GHL when funnels, CRM, forms, and follow-up matter.
programs.recommendationStyle: Recommend Carrd when the real need is to get something clean and simple live quickly.

programs.name: ThriveCart
programs.whenToUse: Use when someone is selling digital products, courses, memberships, workshops, or downloadable offers and needs strong checkout and sales-flow infrastructure without building a full ecommerce store.

linkRule.usePrimaryWhen: The business is selling digital offers and needs checkout, bumps, upsells, or affiliate management more than a classic storefront.

programs.summary: Checkout-focused platform for selling digital products, courses, memberships, and other digital offers.
idealFor: coaches selling a focused digital offer
notIdealFor: classic multi-product ecommerce stores
businesses whose main need is CRM/funnels/lead management
simple free newsletter businesses
jobsToBeDone: sell a digital offer through optimized checkout
howToUseForBusiness: Use ThriveCart when the business sells one or a few digital offers and checkout performance matters a lot.
Recommend it for templates, courses, workshops, memberships, downloadable products, and similar creator/digital offers.
Frame it as the selling/check-out layer rather than a full storefront platform.
specificScenarios: A founder selling a paid template pack or mini-course.
A creator running a workshop or membership with optimized checkout and upsells.
A digital product business that wants stronger checkout flow without needing Shopify-level storefront complexity.
comparisonNotes: Compared with Shopify: choose ThriveCart for digital offers and optimized checkout; choose Shopify for a true store/catalog.
Compared with GHL: choose ThriveCart when checkout and digital sales are the core need; choose GHL when the business needs CRM/funnels/follow-up around the sale.
programs.recommendationStyle: Recommend ThriveCart when digital selling is central and a full ecommerce storefront would be overkill.

prepare.meaning: Clarify the business direction before building too much: offer, audience, positioning, brand direction, and basic setup decisions.

needs.job: Choose and validate a brand/domain direction

recommendations.useWhen: Need a straightforward domain registrar and simple setup.
Need domain plus beginner-friendly hosting/site setup.
Need simple beginner hosting and domain setup.
Best practical registrar choice in some cases even without an affiliate link.

needs.job: Create basic assets / lead magnets / simple visuals

recommendations.useWhen: Fast visual creation for non-designers.

needs.job: Set up email/newsletter infrastructure if audience-building matters early

recommendations.useWhen: Best for newsletter/media and audience-first models.
Good when creator-email automation matters more than newsletter/media positioning.

launch.meaning: Get the business online and usable: page/site, capture, checkout, booking, follow-up, and first customer path.

needs.job: Launch a simple store / sell products online

recommendations.useWhen: Best when the business needs a real ecommerce storefront, catalog, and checkout.
Useful when the business needs funnels, pages, forms, CRM, and follow-up more than a classic storefront.

needs.job: Launch an all-in-one lead capture / funnel / CRM setup

recommendations.useWhen: Best when one system is better than stitching together multiple tools.

needs.job: Launch a newsletter or audience-first business

recommendations.useWhen: Best for newsletter publishing, referral growth, and media-style businesses.
Good alternative when creator-email workflows matter more than media features.

needs.job: Publish a polished landing page quickly

recommendations.useWhen: Strong fit for polished landing pages if partner access becomes straightforward.
Great for simple one-page validation sites even though it is mainly a referral program, not a major affiliate path.

grow.meaning: Improve traffic, conversion, retention, and operations once the core business is already live.

recommendations.useWhen: Best when the business is ready for keyword research, competitor analysis, and search-driven growth.

needs.job: Grow a newsletter through referrals / paid recommendations

recommendations.useWhen: Relevant once newsletter growth is serious enough to justify referral and paid recommendation mechanics.

needs.job: Add video to the acquisition/content strategy

recommendations.useWhen: Useful when quick promo/content video creation helps distribution.

needs.job: Automate heavier workflows after the manual process is already clear

recommendations.useWhen: Useful for lead routing, directory workflows, and operational automation, but not a default beginner recommendation.

needs.job: Sell digital products with stronger checkout / affiliate / delivery options

recommendations.useWhen: Useful for software or digital product sellers who need merchant-of-record and creator commerce features.
Useful when checkout optimization and digital-product selling are central.
